title: Build a Revenue-First Budget Model for Your Consulting Firm
description: Stop budgeting backwards. Build an Excel model that starts with revenue by client, then backs into capacity, hiring, and economics.

steps.title: Recognize why headcount-first budgets lie
steps.content: Most consulting firm budgets start with: headcount x utilization x rates = revenue. That logic is backwards. It tells you what revenue would be if everything went perfectly. It doesn't tell you what revenue will actually be.

steps.title: Start with revenue by client, with timing and probability
steps.content: Build your revenue forecast client-by-client. For each client or engagement: expected revenue, start date, end date, and probability of close. Weight everything. This is your demand signal.

steps.title: Back into required hours and capacity gaps by level
steps.content: From your weighted revenue forecast, calculate how many hours you need at each staff level (partner, senior, associate) to deliver the work. Compare to your current headcount. The gap is your hiring need — driven by demand, not assumptions.

steps.title: Model downside and upside scenarios
steps.content: Show how different revenue scenarios drive different hiring plans. If the pipeline softens, you need fewer hires. If two deals close early, you need to accelerate. A headcount-first model hides this; a revenue-first model exposes it.

steps.title: Expose delivery-mix issues that blended utilization hides
steps.content: Blended utilization looks fine at 75%. But if partners are at 40% and associates are at 95%, you have a delivery-mix problem. The revenue-first model makes this visible because you're matching specific work to specific people.

steps.title: Build the full P&L: revenue > capacity > hiring > comp > OpEx
steps.content: Wire the model end-to-end: revenue drives capacity needs, capacity drives hiring, hiring drives compensation, compensation plus overhead drives your P&L. Every number traces back to a client-level revenue assumption someone can challenge.
